title: The Spiral Scar Where Shores Learned to Loop

features.name: recursive scar
features.desc-on-touch: a cut in the earth spiralling from the shoreline inward, as if the coast tried to repair itself by eating its own edge — you can't see where the scar begins because it loops back to meet itself

features.name: seagull footprints
features.desc-on-touch: eleven-polygon impressions in the sand that keep doubling back, as if the same bird walked the same path at every angle at once — did it? the tracks are fresh and ancient simultaneously

features.name: pinball groove
features.desc-on-touch: a channel worn into the rock by something rolling uphill repeatedly — the ball has been climbing the same spiral for decades, just at different speeds, at different decades

features.name: repair debris
features.desc-on-touch: bits of what might have been a surgical tool, or might have been a shore's own bone — grey metal, salt-corroded, arranged in a circle that meets its own starting point

features.name: mapping tablet
features.desc-on-touch: a stone with a coast drawn on it, but the coast drawn on the stone is exactly the same size as this coast — it's not a map, it's a photograph of photography, territory comparing itself

features.name: the loop-point
features.desc-on-touch: where you're standing; where the scar's end touches its beginning; where repair and damage become the same word; where two clocks showing the same time create a paradox

inscription: the ground accepts your weight but forgets you walked; the coastline loops back to meet itself; repair happened here and nothing is broken anymore, except it's unclear which part was damaged first

file.name: Coastal_Spiral_Damage_Report_1987_1988_1989_Annually_Looping.txt
file.note: yearly assessment of erosion damage that describes the same scar in three different years but using the same language; a coastal photograph that contains multiple exposures layered across decades, all

exits.label: along the curve where the coast catches itself
toward a beach where seagulls render themselves in mirrors
up to the ridge where all spirals converge as one
into the water where the coast teaches itself to swim
